subject: Tort Law
lesson_title: The Standard of Care: The Reasonable Person, Calibrating Factors, and Modified Standards

questions.question: Once a duty of care has been established, what is the standard against which the defendant's conduct is measured in Irish negligence law?

options.A: The objective reasonable person standard — what a reasonable person would have done in the same circumstances
options.B: The subjective standard — what this particular defendant genuinely believed was reasonable
options.C: The professional standard — what a competent professional in the defendant's field would have done
options.D: The statutory standard — whatever standard of conduct is prescribed by the relevant legislation

questions.explanation: The standard of care in negligence is the objective reasonable person standard. It does not ask what this particular defendant believed was reasonable (that would be subjective), but rather what a reasonable person in the defendant's position would have done in the same circumstances. This standard is confirmed in McComiskey v McDermott [1974] and Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Co [1856].

questions.question: In which case did Alderson B provide the classic formulation of negligence as the omission to do something a reasonable man would do, or doing something a prudent and reasonable man would not do?

options.A: Hall v Brooklands Auto Racing Club [1933]
options.B: Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Company [1856]
options.C: Vaughan v Menlove [1837]
options.D: Nettleship v Weston [1971]

questions.explanation: Alderson B's formulation in Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Company [1856] is the classic statement of the reasonable person standard: negligence is the omission to do something which a reasonable man, guided upon those considerations which ordinarily regulate the conduct of human affairs, would do — or doing something which a prudent and reasonable man would not do. This formulation has endured for nearly 170 years.

questions.question: In Hall v Brooklands Auto Racing Club [1933], the reasonable person was described as:

options.A: A person of exceptional care and vigilance who anticipates all foreseeable risks before acting
options.B: The reasonably experienced professional in the defendant's specific area of activity
options.C: The man on the Clapham omnibus — an ordinary, prudent person of average intelligence and reasonable care
options.D: The defendant themselves, applying their own personal standards of judgment and risk assessment

questions.explanation: Hall v Brooklands Auto Racing Club [1933] introduced the famous description of the reasonable person as 'the man on the Clapham omnibus' — a fictional ordinary, prudent citizen of average intelligence and reasonable care. This is not a paragon of perfection nor a reckless fool, but an ordinary person exercising the degree of care that ordinary life requires.

questions.question: Which Irish Supreme Court case is the leading Irish authority on the standard of care in negligence?

options.A: Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989] — articulating the GAP test for all negligence
options.B: Glencar Explorations plc v Mayo County Council [2002] — deriving the standard from the Glencar test
options.C: Breslin v Corcoran [2003] — establishing the standard of the reasonable careful bystander
options.D: McComiskey v McDermott [1974] — confirming the objective standard of the reasonable and careful person in the same circumstances

questions.explanation: McComiskey v McDermott [1974] IR 75 is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on the standard of care. Walsh J confirmed that the standard is what a reasonable and careful person would have done in the same circumstances — an objective, not a subjective, test. FE-1 candidates must cite McComiskey as the primary Irish authority on standard of care.

questions.question: Why is the reasonable person standard in negligence described as 'objective'?

options.A: Because it asks what a reasonable person would have done, without regard to the particular defendant's beliefs, capacity, or personal characteristics
options.B: Because it is determined by reference to objective statutory criteria rather than by the development of case law
options.C: Because it can be measured by expert witnesses who provide objective assessments in each individual case
options.D: Because it applies with equal rigour to all defendants regardless of the specific nature of their activity

questions.explanation: The reasonable person standard is objective because it ignores the defendant's subjective state of mind, personal capabilities, or individual characteristics. The question is not what this particular defendant thought was reasonable, but what an external, objective reasonable person would have done. This prevents defendants from escaping liability by pointing to personal shortcomings.

questions.question: In Nettleship v Weston [1971], to what standard was a learner driver held?

options.A: The standard of the careful beginner, taking into account the learner's known limitations and inexperience
options.B: The standard of the reasonably competent, experienced driver
options.C: A subjective standard based on the learner's personal capabilities and the instructor's own assessment
options.D: A reduced standard, given that the passenger had knowingly accepted the risk of travelling with a learner

questions.explanation: In Nettleship v Weston [1971] QB 691, the English Court of Appeal held that a learner driver is held to the same standard as the reasonably competent experienced driver. The policy rationale is that other road users are entitled to expect the standard the activity demands, regardless of the individual experience of the person performing it. Inexperience does not lower the objective standard.

questions.question: In Vaughan v Menlove [1837], the defendant had below-average intelligence and argued this should lower the standard against which his conduct was judged. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court accepted that the standard should be individually adjusted for persons with below-average intelligence
options.B: The court held that subjective incapacity provides a partial defence, reducing damages proportionately
options.C: The court refused to accept limited personal ability as a defence and applied the uniform objective reasonable person standard
options.D: The court appointed an expert to assess what was reasonable for a person of the defendant's known level of ability

questions.explanation: Vaughan v Menlove [1837] 3 Bing NC 468 is a foundational authority for the objective nature of the standard. The defendant set his haystack on fire through carelessness and argued that he had acted to the best of his own limited ability. The court rejected this argument: the law sets a uniform, objective standard, and personal intellectual limitations are no defence to a negligence claim.

questions.question: In Bolton v Stone [1951], the House of Lords refused to find negligence when a cricket ball struck a pedestrian outside the ground. What was the decisive factor?

options.A: A ball had been hit out of the ground 20 times in the past 30 years, making additional precautions clearly mandatory
options.B: The probability of harm was irrelevant because the pedestrian's injury was severe when it occurred
options.C: Any probability of harm, however small, automatically requires precautions to be taken
options.D: The probability of harm was so low — a ball had only been hit out of the ground six times in thirty years — that no additional precautions were required

questions.explanation: Bolton v Stone [1951] AC 850 established that probability of harm is a key factor in determining breach. Where the probability is extremely low — in Bolton, the ball had escaped the ground only six times in 30 years — the reasonable person is not required to take further precautions. Negligence is not established merely because harm is theoretically conceivable.

questions.question: In Paris v Stepney Borough Council [1951], the House of Lords found the employer negligent for failing to provide protective goggles despite a relatively low probability of injury. Why?

options.A: Because the plaintiff was a one-eyed worker and the gravity of a potential eye injury — complete blindness — was so severe that a reasonable employer would have supplied goggles
options.B: Because goggles were an inexpensive precaution that all employers were required to provide regardless of the nature of the risk
options.C: Because a statutory duty under the Factories Act required all employers to supply protective equipment to one-eyed workers
options.D: Because the probability of injury was actually higher for this plaintiff than for other workers in the same factory

questions.explanation: Paris v Stepney Borough Council [1951] AC 367 established the importance of gravity of potential harm as a calibrating factor. The employer knew the plaintiff had only one eye. Even though the probability of an eye injury was not high, the potential consequence — total blindness — was catastrophic. The House of Lords held that the gravity of the potential harm required the precaution of goggles, even at relatively low probability.

questions.question: What is the significance of the 'social utility' of the defendant's activity as a calibrating factor in the standard of care?

options.A: Activities with high social utility attract the highest standard of care and the most stringent liability
options.B: Where an activity has significant social value, courts are slower to find a breach even where some level of risk is involved
options.C: Social utility operates as a complete defence whenever the defendant was performing a public service at the time of the tort
options.D: Social utility is only relevant in cases involving public authority defendants, not private defendants

questions.explanation: The social utility of the defendant's activity is balanced against the risk created. Where a defendant is engaged in an activity of significant social value — emergency services, medical treatment, construction — the courts take into account that some risk is an inevitable concomitant of socially beneficial activity. This does not provide immunity, but it raises the threshold for finding a breach.

questions.question: In Whooley v Dublin Corporation [1961], the court refused to find negligence when a fire brigade caused an accident while driving at speed to an emergency. What was the reason?

options.A: The fire brigade was immune from negligence liability by statute
options.B: The speed of the vehicle was within the legal maximum prescribed for emergency vehicles
options.C: The social utility of the fire-fighting activity justified the level of risk taken in driving at speed to reach the emergency
options.D: The plaintiff had failed to establish that the risk of an accident was foreseeable by the driver at the time

questions.explanation: Whooley v Dublin Corporation [1961] applied the social utility factor. The court recognised that fire brigades must travel at speed to be effective in saving lives and property. The social utility of emergency fire response was so significant that it justified the increased risk of driving at speed, and therefore no negligence was established against the fire brigade.

questions.question: In Latimer v AEC Ltd [1953], the House of Lords found the employer was not negligent despite a slippery factory floor. Why?

options.A: The employer had complied with all relevant statutory safety regulations governing wet conditions in factories
options.B: The probability of an accident on the wet floor was calculated to be below the legally acceptable threshold
options.C: The cost of providing sawdust to cover the entire floor was prohibitive and could not reasonably have been required
options.D: The employer had used sawdust to treat the floor, and closing the entire factory would have been a disproportionate response to the remaining risk

questions.explanation: Latimer v AEC Ltd [1953] AC 643 applied the cost and practicability factor. The employer had taken the precaution of spreading sawdust on the wet floor after flooding. The House of Lords held that requiring the entire factory to close would have been disproportionate to the level of risk. The employer had taken reasonable precautions given the cost and practicability of alternatives.

questions.question: In Sutherland v Supervalu [2009], the Irish High Court found negligence in the failure to place a wet floor sign. What principle does this illustrate?

options.A: Where a precaution is trivially inexpensive and the injury foreseeable, failure to take that precaution is unreasonable even if the probability of harm is moderate
options.B: Cost is always the decisive factor — any inexpensive precaution must be taken regardless of the probability of harm or its gravity
options.C: Statutory regulations require all retailers to display wet floor signs whenever any risk of slipping exists
options.D: The probability of slipping on a wet floor is so inherently high that social utility can never justify the risk

questions.explanation: Sutherland v Supervalu [2009] illustrates the cost and practicability factor from the opposite angle to Latimer. A wet floor sign was trivially cheap and routinely available. The injury from slipping was foreseeable. In these circumstances, the failure to deploy such a basic and inexpensive precaution was unreasonable. Contrast with Latimer, where the cost of closure was disproportionate to the risk.

questions.question: The 'no hindsight' principle in calibrating the standard of care means that courts must:

options.A: Assess the defendant's conduct with the benefit of all knowledge that has emerged since the time of the negligent act
options.B: Assess the defendant's conduct by reference to what was known and knowable at the time of the act, not by knowledge acquired afterwards
options.C: Apply the standard of the ideal professional looking back on the event with full information available today
options.D: Assume the defendant knew of all risks that were theoretically foreseeable, even if they had not yet been scientifically identified

questions.explanation: The no hindsight principle requires that the defendant's conduct be judged by what was known at the time of the alleged negligence, not by subsequent developments in knowledge or technology. It would be unjust to hold a defendant to a standard that could not have been met at the time — the law asks whether this defendant, with the knowledge then available, acted reasonably.

questions.question: In Roe v Minister for Health [1954], patients were paralysed by contaminated anaesthetic stored in ampoules with invisible cracks. What did the court hold?

options.A: The defendant was strictly liable for injuries caused by anaesthetic products regardless of whether the risk was discoverable
options.B: The defendant was liable because a more cautious method of storage and inspection should have been adopted
options.C: No negligence — the risk of contamination through invisible cracks was unknown at the time, and defendants cannot be required to guard against unknowable risks
options.D: Liability was established because the probability of contamination, though low, was not zero

questions.explanation: Roe v Minister for Health [1954] 2 QB 66 is the leading authority on the no hindsight principle. At the time of the incident, it was not known that anaesthetic in glass ampoules could become contaminated through invisible hairline cracks. The Court of Appeal held that no negligence could be established — defendants cannot be required to guard against risks that were neither known nor knowable at the relevant time.

questions.question: Where a defendant has actual knowledge of a specific, heightened risk in a particular case, what is the effect on the standard of care?

options.A: Knowledge of a specific risk creates strict liability — the defendant is automatically liable for all losses flowing from that risk
options.B: The defendant is entitled to rely on the general reasonable person standard unless the risk was expressly disclosed by the plaintiff
options.C: Specific knowledge of risk is irrelevant — the standard remains the same for all defendants in all circumstances
options.D: The defendant's obligation to take precautions is increased, because a reasonable person with that specific knowledge would take greater care to avoid the harm

questions.explanation: The sixth calibrating factor — actual knowledge of a specific heightened risk — increases the standard of care required. A defendant who knows that a particular plaintiff faces an elevated risk must take additional precautions commensurate with that knowledge. The reasonable person with specific knowledge would do more than the reasonable person without it. This is illustrated by Paris v Stepney, where the employer's knowledge of the plaintiff's disability increased the required standard.

questions.question: What standard of care is applied to a defendant who professes a special skill — for example, a doctor, solicitor, or engineer — in Irish negligence law?

options.A: The standard of the reasonably competent practitioner of that particular skill
options.B: The standard of the ordinary reasonable person applying their best judgment in the circumstances
options.C: The highest standard achievable by the most skilled practitioner in the entire field
options.D: The standard prescribed in the defendant's own professional regulatory code of conduct

questions.explanation: Where a defendant holds out a special skill, the law holds them to the standard of the reasonably competent practitioner of that skill — not the standard of the lay reasonable person. A doctor is judged against the standard of a reasonably competent doctor; a solicitor against a reasonably competent solicitor. This is higher than the general reasonable person standard but does not demand perfection.

questions.question: The Bolam test, established in Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957], provides that:

options.A: A doctor is negligent if any other reasonable doctor in the same specialty would have acted differently in the circumstances
options.B: A doctor is not negligent if they act in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of medical practitioners
options.C: A doctor is held to the standard of the ordinary reasonable person when making broad clinical judgments
options.D: The court must unconditionally accept all expert evidence from medical practitioners without any independent assessment of its logic

questions.explanation: The Bolam test, as stated by McNair J, provides that a medical practitioner is not negligent if they act in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of medical practitioners skilled in that particular art — even if other practitioners would have taken a different approach. In Ireland, this test is not adopted; instead, the Dunne GAP test applies.

questions.question: In Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989] IR 91, Finlay CJ articulated the Irish 'GAP' test. What does GAP stand for, and what crucial qualification was added?

options.A: General and Professional Practice — even an accepted practice is negligent if it causes the patient unnecessary pain or suffering
options.B: General and Acceptable Procedure — accepted practice is not negligent unless it expressly departs from relevant statute or guidelines
options.C: General and Approved Practice — but even an accepted practice may be negligent if it involves an obvious risk that no reasonable practitioner would take
options.D: Generalised and Applied Principle — accepted practice can be rejected by the court without any evidential basis from medical experts

questions.explanation: The Dunne GAP test (General and Approved Practice) holds that a medical professional is not negligent if acting in accordance with a general and approved practice. However, Finlay CJ added the crucial qualification: even a widely accepted practice can be negligent if it involves an obvious risk that no reasonable practitioner would take. This prevents courts from being entirely bound by professional consensus.

questions.question: Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1998] is consistent with the Irish Dunne test because:

options.A: It expanded the Bolam test to impose strict liability on medical practitioners for all procedural errors
options.B: It held that courts must defer entirely to the evidence of responsible bodies of medical practitioners
options.C: It introduced the 'reasonable patient' standard in cases involving informed consent
options.D: It established that a court may reject professional medical opinion that lacks a logical or defensible basis, even if held by a responsible body of practitioners

questions.explanation: Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1998] 1 AC 232 qualified the Bolam test by holding that, while a body of professional opinion may support the defendant's conduct, the court is entitled to reject that opinion if it cannot withstand logical analysis. This is consistent with the Irish Dunne qualification that even a general and approved practice can be negligent if it involves an obvious risk no reasonable practitioner would take.

questions.question: Under the rule in Nettleship v Weston [1971], why is a learner driver held to the standard of the competent driver rather than to a beginner's standard?

options.A: Because other road users are entitled to expect the standard that the activity of driving demands, regardless of the experience of the individual performing it
options.B: Because learner drivers have passed a theory test and are presumed to know the standard applicable to the activity
options.C: Because insurance policies held by learner drivers contractually require them to meet the standard of a fully qualified driver
options.D: Because the law treats all licence-holders as equally experienced once they hold any form of driving licence

questions.explanation: The policy rationale in Nettleship v Weston is that the standard is set by the activity, not the individual. Other road users — pedestrians, cyclists, other drivers — are entitled to expect the standard of care that driving a motor vehicle on a public road demands. It would be unfair to expose them to a lower standard simply because the person behind the wheel is inexperienced.

questions.question: In Mansfield v Weetabix Ltd [1997], a lorry driver suffered a hypoglycaemic episode and caused an accident. He was entirely unaware of his medical condition. What did the court hold?

options.A: The driver was negligent because operating a heavy goods vehicle is a high-risk activity demanding strict adherence to the highest standard of care
options.B: There was no negligence — the driver, being entirely unaware of his condition, could not be judged by the standard of a conscious and fully capable driver
options.C: The driver was liable because any undiagnosed medical condition raises the probability of harm to a level requiring extra precautions before driving
options.D: The driver's employer was vicariously liable regardless of whether the driver himself was personally negligent

questions.explanation: Mansfield v Weetabix Ltd [1997] 1 WLR 1229 established that where a driver suffers a sudden, unforeseeable physical incapacity of which they are entirely unaware, they cannot be held to the standard of the fully conscious and capable driver. It would be unjust to hold a person to a standard they were incapable of meeting without any fault on their part.

questions.question: In Counihan v Bus Atha Cliath [2004], the Irish High Court applied the Mansfield principle. What was the basis for finding the defendant was not negligent?

options.A: The bus had been regularly serviced and maintained to the required standard before the incident occurred
options.B: The bus company had conducted full pre-employment medical screening and found the driver to be fully fit
options.C: The bus driver suffered a sudden, unforeseeable medical episode and had no prior awareness of the relevant medical condition
options.D: The plaintiff had failed to adduce sufficient medical evidence to establish that the driver's condition caused the injuries

questions.explanation: Counihan v Bus Atha Cliath [2004] is the leading Irish application of the sudden incapacity principle. The Irish High Court applied Mansfield and found no negligence because the driver had suffered an unforeseeable medical emergency of which he had no prior knowledge. The case confirms that Irish courts follow the same approach as English courts in sudden incapacity cases.

questions.question: Which of the following accurately states the important qualification to the sudden physical incapacity defence in Irish negligence law?

options.A: Physical incapacity is never a defence in road traffic cases due to the inherent risk posed to other road users
options.B: Physical incapacity is only relevant where the condition developed after the defendant commenced the specific activity in question
options.C: The incapacity defence applies only to employees, not to self-employed or independent contractors
options.D: If a defendant knows of a medical condition that creates risk to others and continues to engage in the dangerous activity, the act of continuing is itself the negligent act

questions.explanation: The crucial qualification to the sudden incapacity defence is knowledge. If a defendant knows — or ought to know — of a condition that creates a risk to others, and chooses to continue the dangerous activity regardless, the negligence lies in the decision to continue, not in the incapacitating event itself. The defence is available only where the incapacity was genuinely unforeseeable to the defendant.

questions.question: What is the significance of Kirby v Burke and Holloway [1944] in Irish negligence law?

options.A: It confirmed that the objective reasonable person standard applies in Ireland
options.B: It introduced the six-factor calibration test for determining whether the standard of care has been breached
options.C: It established that the Bolam professional standard applies to all professions in Ireland
options.D: It held that probability of harm alone, without reference to gravity, is sufficient to establish a breach of the standard of care

questions.explanation: Kirby v Burke and Holloway [1944] IR 207 is a significant Irish authority because it confirmed that the objective reasonable person standard applies in Ireland in the same way as in England. It is cited as early Irish authority for the objective approach to the standard of care in negligence, predating McComiskey v McDermott.

questions.question: Paris v Stepney Borough Council [1951] illustrates which principle about the gravity of potential harm as a calibrating factor?

options.A: A high gravity of potential harm excuses defendants from taking precautions if the probability of that harm is sufficiently low
options.B: Even a low probability of harm may require precautions if the consequences of that harm materialising would be catastrophic
options.C: The gravity of harm factor is only relevant in industrial and workplace negligence cases
options.D: Gravity of harm is assessed with hindsight, after the event, to determine what the appropriate standard should have been

questions.explanation: Paris v Stepney [1951] establishes that probability and gravity are always weighed together. Even where the probability of harm is relatively low, if the potential consequences are catastrophic (in Paris, complete blindness), the reasonable person would take additional precautions. A low probability does not automatically excuse failure to protect against a catastrophic outcome.

questions.question: In what respect does the Irish Dunne GAP test differ from the original English Bolam test?

options.A: Dunne requires expert evidence from a responsible body, whereas Bolam does not require any expert evidence at all
options.B: Bolam completely prohibits courts from finding negligence where any responsible body of practitioners supports the defendant's practice
options.C: Under Dunne, even a widely accepted medical practice can be found negligent if it involves an obvious risk that no reasonable practitioner would accept; the original Bolam test contained no such qualification
options.D: Dunne applies only to gynaecological and obstetric cases, whereas Bolam applies universally to all medical specialisms

questions.explanation: The key difference between Dunne and the original Bolam formulation is the qualification added by Finlay CJ: even a general and approved practice can be negligent if it involves an obvious risk that no reasonable practitioner would take. The original Bolam test was more deferential to professional consensus. This Irish qualification aligns with the later English qualification in Bolitho [1998].

questions.question: What principle do Latimer v AEC Ltd [1953] and Sutherland v Supervalu [2009] together illustrate about the cost and practicability of precautions?

options.A: The law requires all precautions to be taken regardless of cost whenever harm is reasonably foreseeable
options.B: Only precautions required by statute are obligatory — common law does not impose additional cost-based requirements
options.C: Expensive precautions are never legally required, but all cheap precautions must always be taken
options.D: Whether precautions are required depends on proportionality — the cost of the precaution must be weighed against the risk of harm, its probability, and its severity

questions.explanation: Latimer and Sutherland together illustrate the proportionality principle in the cost and practicability factor. In Latimer, closing the factory was disproportionate to the risk (expensive precaution; moderate risk). In Sutherland, a wet floor sign cost almost nothing and was readily available (trivially cheap precaution; foreseeable risk). Whether precautions are required is a matter of proportionality — balancing cost against risk.

questions.question: The no hindsight principle from Roe v Minister for Health [1954] means that:

options.A: A defendant cannot be found negligent for failing to guard against a risk that was not known or knowable at the time of the act
options.B: Courts are legally prohibited from considering what happened after the defendant's act when assessing breach of duty
options.C: Defendants are assessed by the state of knowledge that existed one year before the negligent act occurred
options.D: The no hindsight principle applies exclusively in medical negligence cases and not in ordinary negligence

questions.explanation: The no hindsight principle from Roe v Minister for Health [1954] means that a defendant is assessed by reference to what was known at the time of the alleged negligence, not by knowledge that became available afterwards. Where a risk was genuinely unknown and unknowable at the relevant time, the defendant cannot be held negligent for failing to guard against it.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between probability and gravity of harm in calibrating the standard of care?

options.A: Probability and gravity are assessed entirely independently — a high probability alone always establishes a breach of the standard
options.B: Probability and gravity are always weighed together — a low probability of harm may still require precautions where the potential consequences are catastrophic
options.C: The gravity of harm is irrelevant once the probability is so low that a reasonable person would dismiss the risk entirely
options.D: In all personal injury cases, the law prioritises gravity of harm over probability regardless of the specific circumstances

questions.explanation: Probability and gravity are not assessed in isolation. Bolton v Stone illustrates that very low probability may not require precautions (low probability, moderate gravity). Paris v Stepney illustrates that even a low probability may require precautions where the potential harm is catastrophic (low probability, extreme gravity). The two factors are always weighed together, along with the other calibrating factors.

questions.question: In Bolton v Stone [1951], what was the decisive reason the House of Lords refused to find liability for the cricket club?

options.A: The plaintiff had voluntarily assumed the risk by walking past a cricket ground while a match was in progress
options.B: The club had taken all reasonably practicable precautions by erecting a high perimeter fence around the ground
options.C: The probability of the ball escaping and causing harm was extremely low, given the historical evidence about the frequency of such events
options.D: The cricket club held a statutory exemption from negligence claims under the relevant Sports Facilities legislation

questions.explanation: The decisive factor in Bolton v Stone [1951] AC 850 was the extremely low probability of harm. Evidence showed that in 30 years of use, the ball had only been hit out of the ground on six occasions. Given this very low probability, the House of Lords held that a reasonable person operating the ground would not have been required to take additional precautions beyond those already in place.

questions.question: Which of the following most accurately states the standard applied to a medical professional under the Irish Dunne GAP test from Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989]?

options.A: A medical professional is negligent whenever they cause harm to a patient, regardless of whether any accepted practice was followed
options.B: A medical professional is negligent only if their conduct is criticised by the court, irrespective of the evidence from medical experts
options.C: A medical professional is never negligent if their practice was accepted by any body of practitioners, however small or unrepresentative
options.D: A medical professional is not negligent if acting in accordance with a general and approved practice, unless that practice involves an obvious risk that no reasonable practitioner would accept

questions.explanation: The Dunne GAP test provides: a medical professional is not negligent if acting in accordance with a general and approved practice. However, the crucial qualification is that even such a practice can be negligent if it involves an obvious risk that no reasonable practitioner would take. This is the complete and accurate statement of the test — including the important caveat that courts will not be entirely bound by professional consensus.
